Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
The possibility of artificial consciousness · 20th – 21st c.

Machine Minds

Could a machine have a mind? From Turing’s early framing through contemporary cognitive architectures and robotics, a distinct research community has tried to convert philosophical questions about consciousness into engineering ones. Aleksander, Haikonen, Holland, Chrisley, Shanahan, Reggia, Gamez, Franklin, and Schneider each sketch a different answer.

b. 1937

Igor Aleksander

Depictive neural-state functionalism and the engineering of machine consciousness.

b. 1948

Pentti Haikonen

Phenomenal engineering: designing the internal appearance of information.

contemporary

Owen Holland

Embodied machine consciousness and the robotics of self-models.

contemporary

Ron Chrisley

Synthetic phenomenology: consciousness engineering as a philosophical method.

b. 1960

Murray Shanahan

Global workspace, embodied cognition, and the technological possibility of conscious machines.

contemporary

James Reggia

Computational models of consciousness and the status of simulated minds.

contemporary

David Gamez

A philosophical framework for the scientific study of machine consciousness.

1931 – 2024

Stan Franklin

The LIDA cognitive architecture and the functional approach to machine consciousness.

contemporary

Susan Schneider

Artificial you: consciousness, substrate, and the future of mind.

1898 – 1969

Warren McCulloch

Cybernetic neural form, embodiment, and mind without a ghostly homunculus.

b. 1943

N. Katherine Hayles

Embodiment, information, and a critique of fantasies of disembodied intelligence.

1894 – 1964

Norbert Wiener

Cybernetics, communication, and the moral stakes of animal-machine continuity.

1903 – 1957

John von Neumann

Computation, formal systems, and the psychophysical puzzles at the edge of machine thought.

b. 1954

Rodney Brooks

Embodied robotics and intelligence without detached inner representation.

b. 1948

Edwin Hutchins

Distributed cognition across brains, tools, and socially organized activity.

1927 – 1992 / 1916 – 2001

Allen Newell & Herbert A. Simon

Symbol systems, problem solving, and the architecture of artificial intelligence.

1916 – 2001

Claude Shannon

Information, formal communication, and the limits of mechanized meaning.

1929 – 2017

Hubert Dreyfus

Embodied skill, world-involvement, and a critique of representational AI.

1927 – 2016 / 1928 – 2016

Marvin Minsky & Seymour Papert

Constructive intelligence, society of mind, and anti-homuncular machine cognition.

1923 – 1969

Walter Pitts

Logical neurons and the computational abstraction of nervous activity.

1903 – 1972

W. Ross Ashby

Regulation, variety, and mind as adaptive organization rather than inner essence.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
